name: Akshapada Gautama
dates_notes: The Nyāya-Sūtra is dated by Vidyabhusana (1920, A History of Indian Logic, pp. 50–55) to c. 150 CE on the basis of internal references and pre-Diṅnāga doctrinal stratum; Frauwallner (Geschichte II, pp. 36–46) and Matilal (1977, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, p. 77) accept a similar second-century date for the core layer while recognizing later interpolation. The earliest layers may go back to the first century CE; the latest editorial layer is post-Buddhist-pramāṇa-vāda and may be as late as the third century.
core_thesis: Nyāya names sixteen *padārthas* (categories of discussion) the right discrimination of which yields *niḥśreyasa* (liberation): *pramāṇa*, *prameya*, *saṃśaya*, *prayojana*, *dṛṣṭānta*, *siddhānta*, *avayava*, *tarka*, *nirṇaya*, *vāda*, *jalpa*, *vitaṇḍā*, *hetv-ābhāsa*, *chala*, *jāti*, *nigraha-sthāna* ([Nyāya-Sūtra 1.1.1](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.1)). The foundational text is Akṣapāda Gautama's *Nyāya-Sūtra* in five *adhyāyas*, each *pāda* devoted to one or more of the *padārthas*. Three commitments structure the system. First, *pramāṇa-realism*: the four valid means of cognition — *pratyakṣa* (perception), *anumāna* (inference), *upamāna* (analogical identification), *śabda* (testimony) — are listed at [NS 1.1.3](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.3) and reliable insofar as they are *avyabhicārin* (non-deviating) from their objects. Perception in particular is defined at [NS 1.1.4](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.4) as cognition arising from sense-object contact that is *avyapadeśya* (non-verbal), *avyabhicārin*, and *vyavasāyātmaka* (determinate). Second, the *pañcāvayava* canonical inference: *pratijñā* (thesis), *hetu* (reason), *udāharaṇa* (example), *upanaya* (application), *nigamana* (conclusion) ([NS 1.1.32–39](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.32-39)). The five-membered form is the public, persuasion-bearing inference distinguished from inference for oneself. Third, the doctrine of *abhāva* as a real and cognisable absence, recognized by *anupalabdhi*-licensed perception ([NS 2.2.7–12](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.2.7-12), *abhāva-prāmāṇya-adhikaraṇa*). The *Sūtra* embeds these epistemological commitments within an explicit *mokṣa*-soteriology: discriminating the sixteen *padārthas* breaks the *mithyā-jñāna* → *doṣa* → *pravṛtti* → *janma* → *duḥkha* chain ([NS 1.1.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.2)). The *Sūtra*'s defense of perception against Buddhist *vijñāna-vāda* ([NS 4.2.26–37](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.2.26-37)) and its theory of *śabda-pramāṇa* ([NS 2.1.49–68](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.1.49-68)) are the loci on which the entire later commentarial tradition (Vātsyāyana, Uddyotakara, Vāchaspati, Udayana) builds its mature epistemology.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Sutra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Multi-layered composition; Vidyabhusana 1920 dates the core layer to c. 150 CE with later editorial accretion responding to Buddhist pramāṇa-vāda.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Akṣapāda Gautama by uniform commentarial attestation; the multi-layered character of the text is acknowledged but the ascription of the core to a single author is not in serious dispute.
engaged_works.summary: The foundational *sūtra*-text of Nyāya in five *adhyāyas*, each divided into two *āhnikas*. *Adhyāya* 1 ([NS 1.1–1.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1-1.2)) enumerates and defines the sixteen *padārthas* ([NS 1.1.1](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.1)) and develops the analysis of *pramāṇa* with a long treatment of *pratyakṣa* ([NS 1.1.4](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.4)), *anumāna* ([NS 1.1.5](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.5)), *upamāna* ([NS 1.1.6](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.6)), and *śabda* ([NS 1.1.7–8](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.7-8)). *Adhyāya* 2 ([NS 2.1–2.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.1-2.2)) examines *prameya* in twelve subtypes ([NS 1.1.9](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.1.9), with detailed treatment in [NS 3](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/3)) — *ātman*, *śarīra*, *indriya*, *artha*, *buddhi*, *manas*, *pravṛtti*, *doṣa*, *pretyabhāva*, *phala*, *duḥkha*, *apavarga* — and includes the major *ātman*-existence proofs against Cārvāka and Buddhist no-self positions ([NS 3.1.1–4](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/3.1.1-4) and 3.2.1–9). *Adhyāya* 3 ([NS 3.1–3.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/3.1-3.2)) treats the constitution of *buddhi* and the relations among *manas*, *indriya*, and *ātman*; this is where Nyāya pluralist-realism on the self is established ([NS 3.2.18](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/3.2.18) ff.). *Adhyāya* 4 ([NS 4.1–4.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.1-4.2)) examines *pravṛtti*, *doṣa*, *pretyabhāva*, and *phala*, and gives the theory of *apavarga* (release) ([NS 4.1.58–67](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.1.58-67)); the second *āhnika* ([NS 4.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/4.2)) prosecutes the polemic against Buddhist *vijñāna-vāda*. *Adhyāya* 5 ([NS 5.1–5.2](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/5.1-5.2)) treats *vāda*, *jalpa*, *vitaṇḍā*, *jāti* ([NS 5.1.1–43](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/5.1.1-43)), and *nigraha-sthāna* ([NS 5.2.1–24](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/5.2.1-24)) — the polemical-debate machinery used to prosecute and defeat opponent positions. The *Sūtra* is the base on which the entire later Nyāya commentarial tradition (Vātsyāyana → Uddyotakara → Vāchaspati → Udayana → Gaṅgeśa) is constructed.
